title: Quiz: Classical Monte Carlo Methods
description: Test your understanding of Monte Carlo simulation fundamentals and variance reduction techniques

questions.question: What is the convergence rate of classical Monte Carlo estimation?
questions.explanation: Monte Carlo converges at O(1/√n) due to the Central Limit Theorem. This means to reduce error by half, you need 4× more samples. This is the fundamental limitation quantum computing aims to improve.

questions.question: If you want to reduce Monte Carlo estimation error from 1% to 0.1%, how many times more samples do you need?
questions.explanation: With O(1/√n) convergence, error ∝ 1/√n. To reduce error by 10× (from 1% to 0.1%), you need 10² = 100× more samples. This is why Monte Carlo becomes expensive for high accuracy.

questions.question: How does antithetic variates reduce variance?

options.text: By introducing negative correlation between sample pairs
By sampling from a different distribution

questions.explanation: Antithetic variates uses both Z and -Z random numbers, creating negative correlation between sample pairs. When one sample is high, its antithetic pair is low, reducing the variance of their average.

questions.question: What is the key requirement for using control variates?

options.text: You need a correlated variable with known expectation
The sample size must be very large

questions.explanation: Control variates require a variable X that is correlated with the target Y and has a known expected value E[X]. The adjustment Y - β(X - E[X]) reduces variance while keeping the estimate unbiased.

questions.question: Monte Carlo error increases proportionally with the number of dimensions in the problem.
questions.explanation: False. Monte Carlo's convergence rate O(1/√n) is independent of dimension. This is why MC excels in high-dimensional problems where grid-based methods suffer from the 'curse of dimensionality'.

questions.question: When is importance sampling most effective?

options.text: When estimating rare events or tail probabilities

questions.explanation: Importance sampling is most effective for rare events. By sampling from a distribution shifted toward the region of interest, we get more relevant samples, dramatically reducing variance for tail probability estimation.

questions.question: What theorem provides the theoretical foundation for Monte Carlo convergence?

options.text: Law of Large Numbers and Central Limit Theorem

questions.explanation: The Law of Large Numbers ensures sample means converge to population means, while the Central Limit Theorem describes the distribution of the error (approximately normal with variance σ²/n).

questions.question: Which are valid variance reduction techniques for Monte Carlo? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Antithetic variates, control variates, and stratified sampling are variance reduction techniques that improve efficiency per sample. Increasing sample size reduces error but isn't a 'variance reduction technique' - it's just brute force.
